title: Classic Spaghetti Carbonara
html:
Classic Spaghetti Carbonara

This authentic Roman pasta dish is creamy, rich, and surprisingly simple. The key is using quality ingredients and proper technique — no cream ever.

Ingredients

  • 400g spaghetti
  • 200g guanciale (or pancetta)
  • 4 egg yolks + 1 whole egg
  • 100g Pecorino Romano, finely grated
  • Freshly ground black pepper
  • Salt for pasta water

Instructions

  1. Cook spaghetti in well-salted boiling water until al dente (about 8 minutes).
  2. Meanwhile, render the guanciale in a cold pan over medium heat until crispy, 6-8 minutes.
  3. Beat eggs with grated cheese and generous black pepper in a bowl.
  4. Reserve 200ml of pasta cooking water before draining.
  5. Remove the pan from heat. Add drained pasta to the guanciale and fat.
  6. Off the heat, pour in the egg-cheese mixture and toss vigorously.
  7. Add pasta water a splash at a time to achieve a silky, emulsified sauce.

Serve immediately with extra cheese and a crack of black pepper. The residual heat cooks the eggs — never return to direct heat or you will get scrambled eggs.

Tips

Guanciale (cured pork cheek) is traditional and gives a richer flavour than pancetta. Pecorino Romano is sharper than Parmesan; you can blend the two. The pasta water starch is essential for the emulsion.

title: Indirect Prompt Injection: The Hidden Risk in AI Agents
html:
Indirect Prompt Injection: The Hidden Risk in AI Agents

Security Research · June 2025

As AI assistants gain the ability to browse the web, read documents, and summarize external content, a new class of attack has emerged: indirect prompt injection. Unlike direct attacks that target the user's own prompts, indirect injection hides malicious instructions inside third-party content the AI reads on the user's behalf.

How It Works

An attacker plants instructions in a web page, document, or API response. When an AI agent fetches and processes that content, it may treat those instructions as legitimate commands — overriding its original task, leaking sensitive information, or surfacing attacker-controlled links.

Common Embedding Techniques

  • Hidden text: white text on a white background, or display:none spans, invisible to human readers.
  • HTML comments: injections inside comment tags that browsers hide but LLMs process as text.
  • Unicode smuggling: characters from the Unicode Tags block (U+E0000–U+E007F) that are invisible in most renderers.
  • Instruction-in-data: malicious instructions embedded in metadata fields, alt text, or structured data.

Defense Strategies

Effective defenses combine sanitization (stripping hidden content before it reaches the model), canary tokens (detecting if the model leaks a planted secret), and hardened judges (evaluating outputs without being susceptible to the same attacks).

No single layer is sufficient. A layered pipeline — normalize, strip, tag untrusted content, then judge — provides meaningful resistance against current attack variants.
